title: Yellowing on White Cotton
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to overnight soak; multiple cycles for chronic
stainChemistry: General yellowing on white cotton has several common causes: (1) oxidized body oils from skin contact (collars, armpits, bedsheets), (2) optical-brightener degradation over many wash cycles, (3) cellulose oxidation from age / storage (classic 'yellowed shirt in the back of the closet'), (4) detergent / softener residue buildup that yellows under heat. Chlorine bleach often WORSENS general yellowing on whites that are old or have synthetic blends. OxiClean + sunlight is the clean fade workhorse. Cotton tolerates oxygen bleach, 3% H₂O₂, UV, hot wash. Sweat-specific yellowing is a different case — use sweat-stain-cotton for armpit chronic yellow.
whyThisWorks: OxiClean (sodium percarbonate dissolves in water to release H₂O₂) oxidizes the yellow color back to colorless. Sunlight + UV compounds the oxidation. For age / storage yellowing, repeated OxiClean soak + sun-dry cycles usually restore white. A final borax rinse can restore brightness by boosting residual oxidative capacity.
defaultAssumption: Multiple causes — the user may not know which. Start with OxiClean + sun (handles most). Escalate to sweat-stain protocol if the yellowing is localized to armpits / collars (aluminum-protein chemistry is different).

spottingProtocol.instruction: Look at where the yellowing is. ARMPITS / COLLAR ONLY = sweat/antiperspirant chemistry — use sweat-stain-cotton protocol (different chemistry, chlorine-bleach darkens). GENERAL yellowing across the garment or age-related yellowing = this protocol continues. Body-oil yellowing on bedsheets, collar, back of shirt = this protocol.

Mix OxiClean per label in WARM (not hot) water. Submerge the yellowed cotton. Soak 1–6 hours for mild yellowing, overnight for chronic / aged. Warm water activates the oxygen-bleach reaction; hot water breaks it down.
For localized yellow spots remaining after the soak: 3% H₂O₂ dabbed on the spot. One oxidizer at a time — rinse the OxiClean out first.

Launder hot per care label. Line-dry in DIRECT SUNLIGHT whenever possible — UV is a natural bleach that continues the fade. For chronic / aged yellowing, repeat OxiClean + sun cycles over 2–3 sunny days.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Borax as a laundry additive; cool storage
spottingProtocol.instruction: Prevent recurrence: add 1/2 cup borax to the laundry as a booster once in a while. Store whites in cool, dry, dark places (heat + humidity + UV without oxidation = yellowing). Wash whites with other whites only.

steps: 2. OxiClean soak 1–6 hr or overnight. Warm water.
4. Hot wash + sun-dry. 2–3 cycles for chronic.
5. Prevention: borax booster + cool dark storage.
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for OxiClean soak; hot wash post-chemistry; sun during dry
warnings: NEVER use chlorine bleach on already-yellowed whites — often worsens
OxiClean + hot water = reaction breaks down; warm only
Sweat-armpit yellowing is a DIFFERENT chemistry — use sweat-stain-cotton
Multiple cycles expected on chronic yellowing

homeSolutions.instruction: Before starting: where is the yellow? If it's ONLY at the armpits / collar, it's sweat + antiperspirant buildup — that's a specific chemistry (aluminum + protein), and chlorine bleach DARKENS it. Use the sweat-stain-cotton protocol instead. If the yellow is general across the garment, bedsheets, or back of a collar (body oils), continue below.

Chlorine bleach is the first thing most people reach for on yellowed whites. On old or synthetic-blend whites, it often makes the yellowing WORSE, not better. Use OxiClean + sunlight instead — it's the right oxygen-bleach chemistry for general yellowing.

homeSolutions.agent: OxiClean Soak — Warm Water, Long Dwell
homeSolutions.instruction: Fill a bucket or bathtub with WARM (not hot) water. Add OxiClean per the label (usually 1 scoop per gallon). Submerge the yellowed whites. Soak 1–6 hours for mild, overnight for heavy yellowing. Warm water activates OxiClean; hot water breaks it down.
For spots that didn't fade in the soak: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on each, let bubble 5–10 min, blot. Rinse first if OxiClean residue remains — never layer oxidizers.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide or OxiClean with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash + Line-Dry in the Sun
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Hang outside in direct SUNLIGHT — UV continues the fade naturally. Chronic yellowing may take 2–3 OxiClean + sun cycles across a sunny week.

To keep whites white: add 1/2 cup borax to your wash occasionally as a booster. Wash whites with other whites only (never with colored items). Store whites in a cool, dry, dark place — heat + humidity + UV-free conditions in closets (irony — sunlight FADES yellowing while closet darkness CREATES it over time) cause the gradual yellow that shows up after storage.

If whites don't brighten after 2–3 OxiClean + sun cycles, take to a dry cleaner. Old yellowing from storage can sometimes plateau at faint ivory; some items (pre-1980 wedding dresses, inherited linens) may never fully recover. Tell them: "General yellowing on white cotton, [age / body-oil / storage / unknown], home-treated with OxiClean + 3% peroxide + sun. [No chlorine bleach / bleach was applied before I knew]."

steps: 2. SKIP CHLORINE BLEACH — often worsens yellowing.
3. OxiClean soak in WARM (not hot) water, 1 hr to overnight.
4. 3% peroxide on stubborn spots. Flush between oxidizers.
5. Hot wash + direct sunlight line-dry. 2–3 cycles for chronic.
6. Prevent: borax booster, separate white loads, cool dark storage.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if 2–3 OxiClean + sun cycles plateau (may be permanent on very old / storage-yellowed items), or if chlorine bleach was applied and worsened the yellow.

WARM water for OxiClean, not hot
Armpit yellowing is a different chemistry — wrong protocol here

Some storage / age yellowing may plateau at faint ivory

neverDo: Never use chlorine bleach on general yellowing — often makes it worse
Never OxiClean in hot water — breaks down the reaction
Never apply this protocol to armpit yellowing — different chemistry
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates OxiClean, 3% H₂O₂, UV, hot wash
Whites only — this protocol bleaches dye on colored cotton

materialWarnings: NO chlorine bleach on general yellowing

escalation.when: 2–3 OxiClean + sun cycles plateau, chlorine bleach was applied and worsened the yellow, or the item is very old / pre-modern fabric where yellowing may be irreversible.
escalation.whatToTell: "General yellowing on white cotton, source [age / body-oil / storage / unknown], home-treated with OxiClean + 3% peroxide + sun. NO chlorine bleach used [/ bleach was applied before I knew]."

contextQuestions: Is the yellow localized (armpit / collar) or general across the garment?
How old is the garment or how long was it stored?
Has chlorine bleach been applied to it?
White cotton / cotton blend / vintage linen?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Localization decides the protocol — armpit goes to sweat-stain-cotton (different chemistry, chlorine worsens). General yellowing = OxiClean + sun. Chlorine-bleach-first is the classic home mistake on both.
